The move reflects a broader industry pivot away from single-function note-taking toward comprehensive data retrieval and decision-making platforms, following competitors like Read AI, Fireflies.ai, and Fathom.

## Integrations and Current Capabilities

Otter now allows users to connect Gmail, Google Drive, Notion, Jira, and Salesforce accounts to query data alongside meeting transcripts and summaries. The platform plans to add Microsoft Outlook, Teams, SharePoint, and Slack connections. Beyond search, users can push meeting summaries directly to Notion or draft emails within Gmail. The company has also redesigned its AI assistant to be persistently accessible across the interface, with context-awareness capabilities that adapt to the current screen—whether viewing a specific meeting or channel.

The company simultaneously launched a Windows app supporting botless meeting capture, which records audio from device system inputs rather than deploying a bot into calls. This complements a Mac version released last year, though Otter's CEO Sam Liang noted that enterprise customers still prefer bot-based capture for transparency and shared note distribution among attendees. The company implemented a deduplication feature to prevent multiple bots from joining the same call.

## Market Positioning and Scale

Otter's user base has grown to 35 million, up from 25 million reported last year. The company reported $100 million in annual recurring revenue previously but did not disclose updated financial metrics. The expansion into cross-platform search addresses a core vulnerability in note-taking businesses: transcription and summarization alone have proven insufficient to justify valuations and sustain growth.

By adopting MCP as an industry standard, Otter positions itself within a broader ecosystem shift where AI tools increasingly interoperate through common protocols rather than maintaining isolated silos. This move also reflects defensive positioning against competitors expanding similar capabilities. For enterprises, the ability to search meeting data alongside email, project management, and CRM systems could reduce context-switching, but success depends on adoption breadth and the speed at which additional integrations roll out. The real test is whether unified search actually influences decision-making or becomes another searchable data layer users rarely access.
items.original_title: Otter’s new feature lets users search across their enterprise tools

items.headline: Google Signs Pentagon AI Deal Without Veto Rights on Military Use
items.body:
Google has agreed to a classified deal permitting the US Department of Defense to use its AI models for "any lawful government purpose," according to The Information. The agreement comes less than 24 hours after Google employees publicly urged CEO Sundar Pichai to block Pentagon access, citing concerns about inhumane or harmful applications. The deal places Google in a growing cohort of AI labs willing to work with the US military—alongside OpenAI and xAI—though Anthropic was recently blacklisted by the Pentagon after refusing to remove weapon and surveillance-related safety guardrails from its models.

## Stated Restrictions, Limited Enforcement

The contract reportedly restricts use of Google's AI for domestic mass surveillance and autonomous weapons without human oversight and control. However, the deal explicitly states Google has "no right to control or veto lawful government operational decision-making." This language suggests the restrictions function more as non-binding commitments than enforceable obligations. The agreement also requires Google to make adjustments to its AI safety settings and content filters at the government's request—a provision that effectively grants the Pentagon authority to modify the company's safety mechanisms for military applications.

Google's statement to The Information framed the arrangement as part of existing government contracts and positioned the company within a "broad consortium" supporting national security. The company reiterated its commitment to the public-private consensus against domestic mass surveillance and autonomous weapons without human control, language that closely mirrors the contract's stated terms.

## What the Carve-Out Signals for Enterprise Adoption

The deal reveals a critical gap between public-facing AI safety commitments and actual operational flexibility in government contracts. For enterprises evaluating AI partnerships or compliance frameworks, the Google-Pentagon agreement demonstrates that legal language permitting broad government use—with veto rights explicitly excluded—may become standard in classified defense contracts. The requirement to disable safety filters at government request sets a precedent that could reshape how AI companies approach safety configuration in other sensitive sectors.

The precedent matters because it signals regulatory and contractual pressure on major AI labs to prioritize government access over consistent safety postures. Companies developing AI systems for enterprise customers should anticipate that government demand for modified safety profiles may become a routine contractual expectation. The outcome also indicates that internal employee pressure, when applied publicly, carries limited weight against classified government negotiations.
items.original_title: Google and Pentagon reportedly agree on deal for ‘any lawful’ use of AI
